title: API Handling
description: Backend and ML engineering roles at FAANG require designing resilient API clients. This covers session pooling, retry logic, async HTTP, rate limiting, authentication, and the circuit breaker pattern.

sections.title: Resilient HTTP with requests
sections.description: Raw requests.get() has no retry, no connection pooling, and no timeout. In production, all three are mandatory. Use Session for connection reuse (avoids TCP handshake per request) and HTTPAdapter with Retry for automatic retries on transient failures.
sections.codeBlock:
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter
from urllib3.util.retry import Retry

def create_session(retries=3, backoff_factor=0.5) -> requests.Session:
    session = requests.Session()
    retry = Retry(
        total=retries,
        backoff_factor=backoff_factor,  # sleep: 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 seconds
        status_forcelist=[429, 500, 502, 503, 504],  # retry on these codes
        allowed_methods=['GET', 'POST'],
        raise_on_status=False,
    )
    adapter = HTTPAdapter(max_retries=retry,
                          pool_connections=10,  # connection pools
                          pool_maxsize=50)       # max connections per pool
    session.mount('https://', adapter)
    session.mount('http://', adapter)
    session.headers.update({
        'User-Agent': 'MyService/1.0',
        'Accept': 'application/json',
    })
    return session

# Always set timeout — prevents hanging forever
SESSION = create_session()
response = SESSION.get(
    'https://api.example.com/data',
    timeout=(3.05, 30),  # (connect timeout, read timeout) in seconds
    params={'page': 1, 'limit': 100}
)
response.raise_for_status()  # raises HTTPError for 4xx/5xx

sections.title: Async HTTP — aiohttp
sections.description: For high-throughput workloads (hundreds of concurrent API calls), synchronous requests blocks the thread. aiohttp with asyncio handles thousands of concurrent connections on a single thread via event loop. Use when fetching many URLs in parallel — e.g., scraping, bulk API calls, ML inference pipelines.
sections.codeBlock:
import asyncio
import aiohttp
from typing import Any

async def fetch_one(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, url: str) -> dict:
    async with session.get(url, timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=10)) as resp:
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return await resp.json()

async def fetch_all(urls: list[str], concurrency=50) -> list[Any]:
    semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)  # limit concurrent requests
    async def bounded_fetch(session, url):
        async with semaphore:
            return await fetch_one(session, url)

    connector = aiohttp.TCPConnector(limit=100)  # connection pool
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=connector) as session:
        tasks = [bounded_fetch(session, url) for url in urls]
        # gather: run all concurrently, return all results
        return await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

# Usage
urls = [f'https://api.example.com/item/{i}' for i in range(500)]
results = asyncio.run(fetch_all(urls))
# 500 requests with 50 concurrent — 10× faster than sequential

sections.title: Rate Limiting
sections.description: APIs enforce rate limits (e.g., 100 requests/minute). Violating them returns 429 Too Many Requests. A token bucket is the standard approach: tokens replenish at a fixed rate; each request consumes a token.

sections.title: Authentication — JWT & OAuth2
sections.description:
FAANG APIs use OAuth2 (service-to-service) or JWT (user auth). Know how to implement both client-side.

JWT: Self-contained token with Header.Payload.Signature. Verify signature with public key. Decode payload to get claims (user_id, exp, scopes). Never trust without verifying the signature.
OAuth2 Client Credentials: Machine-to-machine auth. Exchange client_id + client_secret for an access token. Cache the token until expiry.

sections.title: Circuit Breaker Pattern
sections.description: A circuit breaker prevents cascading failures: if a service is failing repeatedly, stop calling it temporarily (open the circuit) and fail fast instead of hammering it with requests. Three states: Closed (normal), Open (fail fast), Half-Open (probe if recovered).

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Requests: HTTP for Humans — The de facto Python HTTP library documentation
  • json — JSON encoder and decoder — Standard library JSON handling
  • FastAPI Documentation — Modern high-performance Python API framework, FAANG standard for ML serving
  • Pydantic Documentation — Data validation using Python type annotations
  • Python API Integration Guide — Real Python tutorial with REST API patterns

details:
REST vs GraphQL: REST: multiple endpoints, fixed response shape, easy caching via HTTP. GraphQL: single endpoint, client specifies exact fields needed, reduces over-fetching. Use REST for simple CRUD APIs; GraphQL for complex data requirements where different clients need different fields (mobile vs desktop). N+1 query problem in GraphQL → use DataLoader (batching).

Pagination patterns: Offset pagination: simple but inconsistent on live data (items shift). Cursor pagination (keyset): stable, efficient for large datasets — cursor = last seen ID or timestamp. Use cursor pagination for infinite scroll and large data sets. FAANG APIs (Twitter, Facebook Graph API) all use cursor-based pagination.

Connection pooling math: Each requests.Session maintains a urllib3 connection pool per host. Pool size should match your concurrency. Rule of thumb: pool_maxsize = max_workers * 1.5. Too small → connections queued. Too large → wasted RAM and OS descriptors.
